dominantFinding: Zero SOC in a state with over 2 lakh ha of degraded grazing commons — livestock development that bypasses pastoral commons governance is a structural mismatch, not a resource constraint. NLM-Fodder proves the model; eleven sibling schemes ignore it.
dominantFailure: 11 of 12 Animal Husbandry schemes deliver individual livestock subsidies on private basis; all fail T1 fundamentally. Individual animals are inherently private excludable goods. Only the NLM-Fodder scheme achieves Near-SOC because it targets grazing commons rather than individual animal ownership.

commonsProfile.keyEcologicalContext: Chhattisgarh's 2+ lakh ha degraded pasturelands support pastoral communities who maintain traditional commons governance practices; individual animal subsidies systematically bypass this institutional foundation

structuralFindings.dominantProblem: The Individual Animal Subsidy Structural Mismatch Problem
crossCuttingPriorities: Formalize Grazing Commons Governance: State-wide survey of grazing commons; formal 'Grazing Community' registrations; codify sustainable grazing limits per block (cost ₹5-8 Cr, 18-24 months)
Implement Sustainable Grazing Monitoring System: Quarterly pasture health monitoring; Grazing Data Platform for real-time extraction+regeneration tracking (cost ₹6-10 Cr, 12-18 months)
Transform Individual Animal Subsidies to Community Livestock Commons: Pilot 100 village-level community dairy cooperatives (Phase 1); expand to 500+ units (Phase 2) — total ₹35-50 Cr, 3 years
Mandatory O&M Fund Earmarking: Reserve 10-15% of capital budgets for 5-year post-project maintenance; hold in community cooperative accounts with performance conditions
contextualFactors: AHD achieves 0% SOC — lowest among T1 departments — due to fundamental structural mismatch between livestock subsidy delivery model and commons governance requirements
The sole Near-SOC scheme (NLM-Fodder) demonstrates that livestock development CAN achieve SOC when organized around shared pasture resources rather than individual animal ownership
Chhattisgarh has significant grazing commons (>2 lakh ha degraded pasturelands) with pastoral communities maintaining traditional commons practices
AHD pathway to SOC requires a 3-year paradigm shift: Year 1 formalize grazing commons governance; Year 2 pilot community livestock cooperatives; Year 3 scale across all blocks

schemes.name: National Livestock Mission (NLM) - Fodder Component
schemes.objective: Develop fodder production infrastructure on community grazing lands, supporting sustainable animal nutrition while maintaining commons-based pasture governance.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (community grazing commons); T1.2=1 (community use right formalized); T1.3~0.65 (≥65% on fodder commons infrastructure); T1.4=1 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (locality: pastoral villages); T2.2~0.50 (landless pastoral HHs); T2.3=1 (Grazing Committee governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (fodder income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.4,000-6,000/HH/year; T3.3=2 (green fodder/storage/animal feed sale); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake via dairy cooperatives)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no mandatory grazing limits); T4.2: undefined; T4.3=0 (no monitoring framework); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Sustainable Grazing Limits: Survey pasture productivity; set carrying capacity per grazing block; establish E/R ≤1.2 (Atal Jal water budgeting model adapted to pastures) — estimated cost ₹8-12 Cr, 12-18 months
Pasture Health Monitoring: Quarterly vegetation assessments; mobile app tracking; early warning system for overgrazing
Replenishment Obligation: Mandatory pasture seeding post-grazing season ≥40% of extraction area annually; link to gram committee benefit disbursement
O&M Fund Earmarking: Reserve 12% of NLM capital for 5-year fodder system maintenance; penalty mechanism (withhold disbursement if quarterly monitoring targets missed)
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No binding sustainable grazing carrying-capacity rule
STRUCTURAL: T4.2 — Pasture extraction/replenishment ratio undefined and unmonitored
STRUCTURAL: T4.3 — No pasture health monitoring framework; score 0 below threshold 1
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance mechanism for pasture sustainability

T1_1.note: Scheme targets community fodder commons on gauchars and community grazing lands. Resource is shared and partially rivalrous.

T1_2.note: Community use right formalized through Grazing Committee; right is community-established rather than statutory under FRA or revenue code.

T1_3.note: Approximately 65% of scheme expenditure flows to fodder commons infrastructure on shared grazing lands. Passes ≥0.50 threshold.

T1_4.note: Fodder commons benefit is partially non-excludable for all pastoral households in the village. Meets minimum non-excludability criterion.

T1_5.note: Traditional fodder harvesting practices from community gauchars are recognized in NLM scheme design and Grazing Committee mandate.

T1_6.note: NLM guidelines mandate community Grazing Committee governance for fodder commons, providing an explicit multi-stakeholder governance layer.

T2_1.note: 75% of beneficiaries are from pastoral villages dependent on grazing commons. Passes ≥0.70 threshold under pastoral definition.

T2_2.note: Landless pastoral and small-holder households are explicitly prioritized in NLM-Fodder targeting. Scheme has explicit pastoral priority mechanism; passes ≥0.30 with targeting.

T2_3.note: Grazing Committee is constituted and functional; provides institutional commons governance for fodder infrastructure management.

T2_4.note: Women membership in Grazing Committees is encouraged under NLM operational guidelines, enhancing inclusive commons governance.

T2_5.flag: No formal intergenerational knowledge transfer mechanism for traditional pastoral practices embedded in scheme design
T2_5.note: Scheme lacks a formal provision for documenting or transmitting traditional pastoral management knowledge across generations.

T3_1.note: NLM-Fodder mandates a livelihood income output from fodder commons; income generation from fodder sale and animal productivity is a scheme objective.

T3_2.note: Observed Delta-Y midpoint of Rs.5,000/HH/year from fodder sales and improved animal productivity. Passes ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Three documented income pathways from the fodder commons. Score 2 reflects multiple realized income channels.

T3_4.note: Institutional offtake established via dairy cooperatives and local market linkages. Score 1; offtake mechanism operational.

T3_5.flag: No systematic income tracking MIS at commons level; outcome data relies on scheme-level reports rather than household income monitoring
T3_5.note: Absence of a dedicated MIS for household income outcomes from fodder commons limits verifiability of Delta-Y at scale.

T4_1.note: No binding grazing carrying-capacity rule is prescribed in NLM-Fodder operational guidelines. Partial sustainability intentions exist but no enforceable limit.

T4_2.note: Pasture extraction-to-replenishment ratio is undefined and unmonitored under the scheme. No rho measurement framework exists.

T4_3.note: No pasture health monitoring framework; no vegetation or biomass metrics tracked. Score 0 falls below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No dedicated O&M fund is earmarked for fodder infrastructure maintenance. Post-project sustainability not secured.

T4_5.note: No intangible stewardship mechanism or physical O&M obligation is embedded in scheme conditionalities.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven commons governance for grazing sustainability
T4_6.note: Grazing Committee is constituted for management but lacks binding sustainability governance powers over extraction limits.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department is not in a climate-stressed zone designation; T4.7 stress override not triggered.

references.title: National Livestock Mission — Operational Guidelines, Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry & Dairying
NLM Fodder Component Implementation Framework — DAHD Annual Report 2023-24
Chhattisgarh AHD — NLM Fodder Scheme Progress Report, State Implementation Unit

schemes.name: Government Livestock & Poultry Farms
schemes.objective: Establish government-owned livestock and poultry breeding farms for genetic improvement and supply of quality breeding stock to individual farmers.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual animal breeding stock — private excludable good); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.05 (≥90% on breeding stock development); T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no livelihood output mandated); Delta-Y: negligible (indirect benefit); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Community Livestock Commons Model: Form gram-level units (30-50 co-owned households); gram committee manages breeding bulls/buffalo as commons asset
Commons Grazing Integration: Community herd commons for genetics improvement; benefit-sharing through community revenue
Governance Mandate: Gram Sabha manages breeding program; SC/ST and women representation mandated
Income Target: Community breeding cooperative must achieve ₹5,000+/HH/year from milk/meat sales — estimated redesign cost ₹15-20 Cr
governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No recognition of indigenous cattle commons practices
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance

T1_1.note: Scheme produces and distributes individual breeding stock — a private excludable good. No commons resource is targeted or managed.

T1_2.note: No commons use right established. Breeding stock is transferred to individual farmers as private property with no shared access mechanism.

T1_3.note: Less than 5% of scheme expenditure touches any shared resource; over 90% funds government breeding farm infrastructure serving private beneficiaries.

T1_4.note: Breeding stock is fully excludable; each unit of benefit accrues to a single identified farmer. No non-excludability present.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of indigenous cattle commons practices or traditional breed stewardship in scheme design
T1_5.note: Government farm model is technocratic; traditional pastoral knowledge and community breed stewardship are entirely absent.

T1_6.note: No community governance layer exists; scheme is fully department-administered with no gram-level institutional participation.

T2_1.note: Beneficiaries are dispersed individual farmers; locality concentration at 50% falls below ≥0.70 pastoral commons threshold.

T2_2.note: No SC/ST or marginal group priority mechanism. Demographic incidence estimated at 20%; no explicit targeting. Pass:false as no priority mechanism.

T2_3.note: No commons governance body. Government farm is managed by AHD staff with no community institutional involvement.

T2_4.flag: No gender inclusion provision in farm management or beneficiary selection
T2_4.note: Scheme has no mandated women's participation in governance or preferential targeting for women-headed households.

T2_5.flag: No intergenerational knowledge transfer mechanism for traditional livestock breeding practices
T2_5.note: Government farm model excludes indigenous breed knowledge and community pastoral expertise from scheme design.

T3_1.note: No livelihood income output is mandated. The scheme objective is breed supply, not household income generation.

T3_2.note: Projected indirect income gain from improved genetics is negligible (~Rs.1,000/HH/year). Falls far below ≥5,000 projected threshold. Provisional estimate.

T3_3.note: No documented income pathway from the scheme. Benefit is indirect genetic improvement without any income generation mechanism.

T3_4.note: No offtake mechanism established. Productivity gains are not linked to any market or cooperative procurement channel.

T3_5.flag: No income outcome tracking; no MIS for beneficiary household income measurement
T3_5.note: Scheme has no framework to track whether genetic improvement translates to measurable household income improvement.

T4_1.note: No sustainable grazing or resource extraction rule. Government farm operates independently of any pastoral commons management framework.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio is not applicable and undefined. Pass:false as no sustainability metric exists.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring of any resource. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund earmarked. Farm infrastructure maintenance is entirely dependent on annual government budget allocations.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism exists. Scheme creates no obligation for resource maintenance or sustainable use.

T4_6.note: No community governance mechanism for any aspect of sustainability or commons stewardship.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone designation; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Chhattisgarh Animal Husbandry Department — Government Livestock Farm Scheme Guidelines
Chhattisgarh State Budget 2024-25 — Animal Husbandry Department Allocation
DAHD Annual Report 2023-24 — State Livestock Farm Performance

schemes.name: Artificial Insemination (AI) Program
schemes.objective: Provide AI services to farmers for genetic improvement of livestock without on-farm breeding animals, improving milk yield and herd productivity.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual animal genetics — private good); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.10; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no income mandate); Delta-Y: Rs.1,000-2,000/year (indirect yield increase); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Redesign as Community Livestock Genetics Commons: Community AI service cooperatives with shared infrastructure; individual farmer participation through cooperative membership
Commons Governance: Gram cooperative manages AI center; all village livestock owners eligible; benefit-sharing through cooperative profit
Sustainability: Maintain genetic diversity through rotation protocols; quarterly animal health monitoring
Income Mandate: Cooperative must demonstrate ₹5,000+/member/year productivity gain within 3 years
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Scheme targets individual animal genetics; inherently private excludable good
MISSING: T2.2 — No SC/ST or marginal household priority targeting mechanism
MISSING: T3.1 — No livelihood income mandate embedded in scheme design

T1_1.note: AI services improve individual animal genetics — a fully private excludable benefit. No shared commons resource is implicated.

T1_2.note: No commons use right. AI service is delivered to individual animal owners; no shared access or community tenure established.

T1_3.note: Around 10% of expenditure could be attributed to shared service infrastructure (AI centers), but primary benefit delivery is private.

T1_4.note: Genetic benefit accrues exclusively to the individual cow/buffalo owner; fully excludable with no spillover to commons.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of traditional cattle breeding knowledge or community herd management practices
T1_5.note: Scheme is a technical service delivery model; traditional livestock knowledge and community pastoral practices are not integrated.

T1_6.note: No community governance structure. AI program is delivered by government veterinary staff to individual beneficiaries.

T2_1.note: Beneficiaries are dispersed individual cattle owners; 60% locality concentration falls below ≥0.70 pastoral commons threshold.

T2_2.note: No SC/ST priority mechanism. Demographic incidence estimated at 20%; no explicit targeting mechanism. Pass:false.

T2_3.note: No community governance body. AI program is fully department-administered.

T2_4.flag: No women's representation or gender-preferential targeting in AI service delivery
T2_4.note: Scheme has no provision for women's leadership in service governance or priority access for women livestock owners.

T2_5.flag: No intergenerational knowledge or indigenous livestock breeding practice embedded in scheme
T2_5.note: Technical AI service model replaces rather than integrates traditional community livestock management knowledge.

T3_1.note: No income mandate. Scheme aims to improve yield indirectly; no income generation target is set or monitored.

T3_2.note: Projected indirect income from yield improvement estimated at Rs.1,500/HH/year midpoint. Below ≥5,000 projected threshold.

T3_3.note: No documented income pathway. Benefit is indirect yield gain without any formalized income generation or market linkage.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake mechanism. Productivity gains are not connected to any procurement channel.

T3_5.flag: No income outcome tracking or MIS for measuring household productivity gains
T3_5.note: No framework to verify whether AI-induced yield improvements translate to measurable household income.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule. AI program has no connection to grazing commons management or sustainable resource extraction obligations.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed under this scheme; rho ratio undefined and not applicable.

T4_3.note: No ecological or resource health monitoring. Score 0 falls below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund. AI program relies on recurring government budget for equipment and semen maintenance.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Scheme creates no obligation for resource sustainability or commons management.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven governance for any sustainability dimension
T4_6.note: Scheme lacks any community governance mechanism that could enforce sustainable practices.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: National Artificial Insemination Programme — DAHD Operational Guidelines
Chhattisgarh AHD — AI Programme State Implementation Report
Livestock Census 2019 — Chhattisgarh State Report, DAHD

schemes.name: Backyard Poultry Scheme
schemes.objective: Provide low-cost poultry rearing support to small farmers and landless households for egg/meat production and household income.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual household poultry — private good); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.05; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no income mandate); Delta-Y: Rs.2,000-3,000/year; T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Transform to Community Poultry Commons: Village-level production units (30-50 households); gram committee manages breeding stock, vaccination, and feed commons
Benefit-Sharing: Community poultry cooperative distributes egg/meat income; SC/ST and women priority in committee leadership
Income Target: Cooperative must achieve ₹5,000+/member/year from poultry sales
Sustainability: Annual flock health monitoring; mandatory biosafety protocol compliance
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Backyard poultry is a private household good; no commons resource involved
MISSING: T2.2 — No explicit SC/ST priority mechanism despite high demographic incidence
MISSING: T3.1 — No income mandate; income is incidental not mandated

T1_1.note: Backyard poultry is entirely individual household property — a private excludable good. No shared commons resource is involved.

T1_2.note: No commons use right established. Poultry units are individual household property with no shared access or community tenure.

T1_3.note: Approximately 5% of expenditure may relate to shared training or vaccination inputs; over 90% is direct private asset transfer.

T1_4.note: Poultry benefit is fully excludable; each bird accrues exclusively to the individual recipient household.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of traditional community poultry or small livestock commons practices in tribal areas
T1_5.note: Scheme design ignores existing community-level poultry management practices common in tribal Chhattisgarh.

T1_6.note: No community governance layer. Scheme is direct department-to-individual delivery with no gram-level institutional involvement.

T2_1.note: 75% of beneficiaries are village-level small and marginal farmers. Passes ≥0.70 threshold but T1 failure means T2 overall fails.

T2_2.note: High demographic incidence of SC/ST (~50%) but no explicit priority targeting mechanism. Pass:false as no mechanism to ensure priority access.

T2_3.note: No community governance institution. Scheme delivers directly to individual households without gram committee oversight.

T2_4.flag: No formal women's representation or gender-targeted priority in poultry scheme design
T2_4.note: Despite women's traditional role in backyard poultry, scheme has no mandated women's leadership or targeting provision.

T2_5.flag: No intergenerational knowledge transfer for traditional community poultry practices
T2_5.note: Scheme does not integrate traditional poultry management knowledge prevalent in tribal communities.

T3_1.note: No livelihood income mandate. Egg and meat income is incidental to scheme delivery but not a stated or monitored target.

T3_2.note: Projected Delta-Y midpoint Rs.2,500/HH/year from egg and meat sales. Falls below ≥5,000 projected income threshold.

T3_3.note: No documented income pathway formalized in scheme design. Income is a by-product, not a designed outcome.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake. Eggs and meat sold through informal local market with no cooperative or procurement linkage.

T3_5.flag: No income tracking MIS; no household-level income outcome measurement
T3_5.note: Scheme has no monitoring framework to measure whether poultry support translates to sustained household income.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule applicable. Backyard poultry has no connection to any shared natural resource or commons extraction.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable to this scheme.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund. Individual poultry units are self-maintained by households with no collective maintenance provision.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Scheme creates no obligation for any shared resource management.

T4_6.note: Scheme entirely lacks community governance for any sustainability dimension.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Chhattisgarh Backyard Poultry Development Scheme — AHD Guidelines
DAHD — Poultry Development in Tribal Regions, Ministry Report 2023
Chhattisgarh AHD Annual Budget Statement 2024-25 — Poultry Scheme Allocation

schemes.name: Dairy Development Scheme
schemes.objective: Provide subsidies for dairy unit construction (cattle shed, milk storage, processing equipment) enabling individual farmers to establish dairy operations.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual dairy unit on private land); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.10; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (milk income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000-12,000/year from milk sales; T3.3=1 (single pathway); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake via dairy cooperatives)

upgradePathway: Transform to Community Dairy Commons: Establish village dairy cooperatives for 30-50 farmers; individual farmers provide milk; cooperative handles processing/marketing
Commons Governance: Benefit-sharing through cooperative profits; SC/ST and women representation mandated
Grazing Commons Integration: Link dairy subsidy to participation in community grazing commons management
Sustainability: Cooperative must maintain grazing commons health; annual pasture monitoring as continuation condition
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Individual private dairy unit on private land; no commons resource
STRUCTURAL: T2.2 — No SC/ST explicit priority mechanism; dairy subsidy reaches better-off land-holding farmers
MISSING: T4.1 — No grazing commons sustainability rule linked to dairy subsidies

T1_1.note: Individual dairy unit constructed on private land — a fully private excludable capital asset. No commons resource targeted.

T1_2.note: No commons use right. Cattle shed and equipment are private property of subsidy recipient with no shared access.

T1_3.note: About 10% of expenditure relates to shared cooperative linkage facilitation; 90% funds private dairy infrastructure.

T1_4.note: Dairy unit benefit is fully excludable to the individual farmer-owner. No non-excludable commons benefit generated.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of community dairy commons practices or traditional cooperative milk-sharing arrangements
T1_5.note: Scheme design ignores existing community-level dairy management traditions in rural Chhattisgarh.

T1_6.note: No community governance layer. Dairy subsidy is individual-to-department with no gram-level commons institution.

T2_1.note: 70% of beneficiaries are village-level farmers; borderline pass at exactly ≥0.70 threshold. But T1 failure makes T2 overall fail.

T2_2.note: SC/ST incidence estimated at 20% but no explicit priority mechanism. Dairy subsidies tend to reach land-holding farmers. Pass:false.

T2_3.note: No community governance body for dairy scheme. Milk cooperative linkage exists in some areas but is not a scheme governance requirement.

T2_4.flag: No women's leadership provision in dairy subsidy governance or cooperative management mandate
T2_4.note: Scheme lacks mandated women's representation in any cooperative or management body despite women's central role in dairy work.

T2_5.flag: No intergenerational knowledge transfer for traditional community dairy practices
T2_5.note: Scheme focuses on physical infrastructure without integrating community dairy management knowledge.

T3_1.note: Milk income is a mandated scheme outcome; dairy unit viability requires sustained milk production and sale.

T3_2.note: Observed Delta-Y midpoint Rs.10,000/HH/year from milk sales. Clearly passes ≥3,000 observed income threshold.

T3_3.note: Single documented income pathway from dairy unit. Score 1 reflects one operational income channel via milk sales.

T3_4.offtakeMechanism: State dairy cooperative procurement at MSP
T3_4.note: Institutional offtake through state dairy cooperative at minimum support price. Mechanism is operational and documented.

T3_5.flag: No household income tracking MIS; cooperative procurement data not systematically linked to scheme monitoring
T3_5.note: Scheme lacks integrated income outcome monitoring linking dairy cooperative procurement to individual beneficiary income measurement.

T4_1.note: No sustainable grazing rule. Dairy scheme does not link to any grazing commons management obligation despite dairy cattle depending on pasture.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable to this individual dairy infrastructure scheme.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund earmarked. Dairy shed maintenance is entirely farmer's individual responsibility after subsidy disbursement.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism linking dairy subsidy to any shared resource sustainability obligation.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven governance linking dairy subsidies to grazing commons sustainability
T4_6.note: Individual dairy units are disconnected from any grazing commons governance framework.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Chhattisgarh Dairy Development Scheme — State AHD Guidelines
National Dairy Development Board — State Cooperative Procurement Data 2023-24
NABARD — Dairy Entrepreneurship Development Scheme Evaluation Report

schemes.name: Veterinary Services
schemes.objective: Provide government veterinary clinics and field services for livestock healthcare, disease diagnosis, vaccination, and extension services.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (healthcare is service to private livestock); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.15; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (public health service, not income mandate); Delta-Y: Rs.1,000-2,000/year (disease prevention savings); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Note: This is a PUBLIC HEALTH INPUT SERVICE, not a livelihood scheme. Falls outside direct SOC framework scope.
SOC Convergence: Link veterinary access to community grazing commons participation — condition priority service on Grazing Committee membership
Commons Monitoring Integration: Veterinary data (disease patterns) integrated with commons health monitoring platform
Community Health Extension: Train community livestock health workers (one per Grazing Committee) to reduce dependency on government vets
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Veterinary services treat private livestock; inherently private good service
MISSING: T2.2 — No explicit SC/ST priority in service delivery despite high incidence
MISSING: T3.1 — Public health service does not have income mandate by design

T1_1.note: Veterinary healthcare is a service to individually-owned private livestock. No commons resource is managed or targeted.

T1_2.note: No commons use right. Service is delivered to individual animal owners; no shared access or community tenure established.

T1_3.note: Approximately 15% of expenditure relates to shared clinic infrastructure accessible to multiple households, but primary benefit is private.

T1_4.note: Veterinary benefit accrues to individual livestock owner; no non-excludable commons benefit generated.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of traditional livestock health practices or community herbal remedy knowledge in service design
T1_5.note: Government veterinary model does not integrate traditional community knowledge of animal health management.

T1_6.note: No community governance structure. Veterinary services are government-staff delivered with no gram-level institution.

T2_1.note: 80% of service access is at village level; high geographic reach. But T1 failure and T2_2 failure make T2 overall fail.

T2_2.note: SC/ST incidence estimated at 50% by demographic composition, but no explicit priority service mechanism. Pass:false as no targeting mechanism.

T2_3.note: No community governance body. Veterinary services are fully government-administered with no community institution.

T2_4.flag: No women's representation in veterinary service governance or women livestock health worker program
T2_4.note: Scheme lacks any provision for women's leadership in veterinary service governance or community health extension.

T2_5.flag: No integration of traditional animal health knowledge in veterinary extension program
T2_5.note: Extension services rely entirely on modern veterinary protocols without incorporating community traditional practices.

T3_1.note: Veterinary services are a public health input; no livelihood income mandate. Disease prevention savings are indirect and untracked.

T3_2.note: Projected disease-prevention savings estimated at Rs.1,500/HH/year midpoint. Falls below ≥5,000 projected threshold.

T3_3.note: No formalized income pathway. Veterinary services create no direct income generation mechanism.

T3_4.note: No offtake mechanism. Veterinary services do not connect to any market or procurement channel.

T3_5.flag: No income outcome tracking; disease prevention savings not measured at household level
T3_5.note: Scheme has no framework to quantify whether veterinary interventions translate to measurable household income protection.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule. Veterinary services have no connection to grazing commons management or sustainable resource use.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund. Veterinary clinic maintenance depends entirely on annual government budget provision.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Service creates no obligation for shared resource management.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven governance for veterinary service sustainability
T4_6.note: No community governance mechanism linking veterinary access to commons stewardship.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Chhattisgarh AHD — Veterinary Services and Polyclinics Scheme Guidelines
DAHD — Livestock Health Programme Annual Report 2023-24
Chhattisgarh Veterinary Infrastructure Budget Estimate 2024-25

schemes.name: Gaushan Palan Yojana
schemes.objective: Provide financial support and training for individual farmers to adopt sustainable cattle rearing practices with emphasis on indigenous and heritage cattle breeds.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual cattle on private farm); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.10; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no income mandate); Delta-Y: Rs.2,000-3,000/year; T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Transform to Community Cattle Commons: Gram-level community cattle units (30-40 cows) managed by community dairy cooperative; heritage breed conservation as commons responsibility
Commons Governance: Individual farmers provide labor and training participation; gram committee manages herd
Benefit-Sharing: Income through cooperative milk sales; heritage breed premium distribution
Cultural Conservation: Document indigenous breed knowledge; community-managed genetic preservation program
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Individual cattle on private farm; no commons resource targeted
STRUCTURAL: T2.1 — 65% locality concentration falls below ≥0.70 pastoral commons threshold
MISSING: T2.2 — No SC/ST priority mechanism; 45% incidence without explicit targeting

T1_1.note: Individual cattle reared on private farm — a private excludable asset. No commons resource is managed or involved.

T1_2.note: No commons use right established. Cattle and shelter are individual private property with no shared access mechanism.

T1_3.note: Approximately 10% of scheme expenditure relates to shared training and extension; remaining 90% funds individual cattle support.

T1_4.note: Cattle rearing benefit is fully excludable to the individual farmer. Heritage breed conservation intent does not create commons benefit.

T1_5.flag: Heritage breed conservation focus could integrate traditional community cattle commons knowledge but scheme design does not do so
T1_5.note: Despite indigenous breed emphasis, scheme does not systematically document or institutionalize traditional community cattle management knowledge.

T1_6.note: No community governance structure. Scheme is individual farmer support with no gram-level institution managing the cattle.

T2_1.note: 65% locality concentration falls below the ≥0.70 pastoral commons threshold. Beneficiaries are spread across dispersed individual farms.

T2_2.note: SC/ST demographic incidence at 45% but no explicit priority mechanism. Pass:false as targeting mechanism absent.

T2_3.note: No community governance body. Scheme is individual farmer-focused with no gram committee or commons institution.

T2_4.flag: No women's participation mandate despite women's primary role in cattle care in rural Chhattisgarh
T2_4.note: Scheme lacks any provision for women's leadership in cattle management governance or preferential targeting for women farmers.

T2_5.flag: Heritage breed emphasis not linked to systematic community knowledge documentation program
T2_5.note: Indigenous breed support lacks framework for capturing and preserving traditional community knowledge about heritage cattle management.

T3_1.note: No income mandate. Scheme supports cattle rearing practices without setting or monitoring household income targets.

T3_2.note: Projected Delta-Y midpoint Rs.2,500/HH/year from improved cattle productivity. Falls below ≥5,000 projected threshold.

T3_3.note: No documented income pathway. Productivity improvement is assumed but no income generation mechanism is formalized.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake. Milk and cattle product sales are through informal local markets without cooperative linkage.

T3_5.flag: No income tracking; heritage breed premium income not measured or monitored
T3_5.note: Scheme lacks any MIS to track whether heritage breed support generates measurable income premiums for farmers.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule linked to grazing or resource extraction. Cattle rearing scheme has no commons resource governance obligation.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable to this individual cattle scheme.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund. Individual cattle maintenance is entirely the farmer's responsibility after scheme support ends.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Scheme creates no obligation for shared resource management or commons sustainability.

T4_6.flag: No community governance for heritage breed conservation sustainability
T4_6.note: Absence of any community governance mechanism means heritage breed conservation has no institutional sustainability foundation.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Chhattisgarh Gaushan Palan Yojana — State AHD Scheme Guidelines
National Gokul Mission — Indigenous Breed Conservation Framework, DAHD
Chhattisgarh Indigenous Cattle Breeds Study — NABARD 2022

schemes.name: Rajasthan Goat Breeding Scheme (RGBS adapted)
schemes.objective: Promote goat rearing through subsidy and supply of improved breeding stock, supporting individual farmers in goat-rearing livelihoods.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual goat ownership); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.10; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no income mandate); Delta-Y: Rs.3,000-5,000/year; T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Transform to Community Goat Commons: Gram-level community goat herds (50-100 goats) managed by pastoral group cooperative
Commons Governance: Individual participation in herding/management labor; grazing commons formalized
Benefit-Sharing: Through pastoral cooperative; SC/ST and women representation mandated
Sustainability: Grazing limits (E/R ≤1.2); quarterly pasture health monitoring; mandatory seeding post-grazing
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Individual goat ownership is a private excludable good; no commons resource involved
MISSING: T2.2 — No explicit SC/ST priority mechanism despite high demographic incidence
STRUCTURAL: T3.2 — Projected income Rs.4,000/HH/year falls below ≥5,000 threshold; scheme insufficient even if redesigned with income mandate

T1_1.note: Individual goat ownership and breeding stock are private excludable goods. No commons resource is involved in scheme design.

T1_2.note: No commons use right. Goats and breeding stock are individual private property transferred through subsidy.

T1_3.note: Approximately 10% of expenditure relates to shared breeding infrastructure; primary expenditure funds individual goat and breeding stock transfer.

T1_4.note: Goat benefit is fully excludable to individual farmer-owner. No non-excludable commons benefit generated.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of traditional pastoral goat herding commons practices despite goat herding being a community activity in many areas
T1_5.note: Scheme treats goat rearing as individual farming rather than recognizing existing community herding commons in pastoral areas.

T1_6.note: No community governance layer. Breeding stock distribution is direct department-to-individual delivery.

T2_1.note: 70% of beneficiaries are village-level pastoral farmers; borderline pass. But T1 failure means T2 overall fails.

T2_2.note: SC/ST demographic incidence ~50% but no explicit priority mechanism. Pass:false as no targeting mechanism to ensure priority access.

T2_3.note: No community governance body. Scheme is individual farmer subsidy with no community institution involved.

T2_4.flag: No women's priority in goat scheme despite women's traditional role in small ruminant management
T2_4.note: Scheme lacks any provision for women's leadership or preferential access despite women's central role in goat rearing.

T2_5.flag: No integration of traditional community herding routes or pastoral commons practices in scheme design
T2_5.note: Scheme ignores traditional community herding practices and commons grazing routes central to pastoral goat rearing.

T3_1.note: No income mandate. Scheme provides breeding stock subsidy without setting or monitoring household income generation targets.

T3_2.note: Projected Delta-Y midpoint Rs.4,000/HH/year from goat sales and meat income. Falls below ≥5,000 projected threshold. Provisional estimate.

T3_3.note: No documented income pathway. Goat sale and meat income are assumed outcomes without any formalized mechanism.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake. Goat sales through informal local markets without cooperative or procurement linkage.

T3_5.flag: No income tracking MIS; goat productivity and income gains not monitored at household level
T3_5.note: Scheme has no framework to measure whether improved breeding stock translates to sustained household income improvement.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule. Goat breeding scheme has no link to grazing commons management or sustainable resource extraction.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund. Individual goat herd maintenance is entirely the farmer's responsibility after scheme subsidy.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Scheme creates no obligation for shared resource or commons management.

T4_6.flag: No community governance for grazing sustainability despite goat herding pressure on pasturelands
T4_6.note: No community governance to manage the grazing pressure that subsidized goat expansion creates on shared pasturelands.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Rajasthan Goat Breeding Scheme — DAHD Adaptation Guidelines for State Implementation
Chhattisgarh RGBS Adaptation — State AHD Implementation Order
Small Ruminant Development in Central India — NABARD Evaluation 2022

schemes.name: Rastriya Gokul Mission (State Implementation)
schemes.objective: Genetic up-gradation of bovine population through superior breeding stock and genetic improvement programs, supporting state cattle productivity goals.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual animal genetics); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.10; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no income mandate); Delta-Y: Rs.1,000-2,000/year (indirect); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Redesign as Community Cattle Genetics Commons: Gram-level cooperative breeding management; genetic improvement benefit becomes commons property
Commons Governance: Individual farmers participate through cooperative; all members benefit from improved genetics
Benefit-Sharing: Dairy cooperative distributes productivity gain; milk price premium from superior genetics
Sustainability: Genetic diversity protocols; maintain heritage breeds alongside improved stock in community herd
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Genetic improvement targets individual animal productivity; inherently private good
MISSING: T2.2 — No SC/ST priority mechanism in Gokul Mission state implementation
MISSING: T3.1 — No income mandate; mission is genetic productivity focused without livelihood income target

T1_1.note: Rastriya Gokul Mission targets individual bovine genetic improvement — a private excludable good benefiting individual cattle owners.

T1_2.note: No commons use right. Genetic benefits accrue to individual cattle owners; no shared commons access established.

T1_3.note: About 10% of expenditure relates to shared Gokul Gram infrastructure; primary expenditure funds individual animal genetic improvement.

T1_4.note: Genetic improvement benefit is fully excludable to individual animal owner. No non-excludable commons benefit.

T1_5.flag: Mission claims indigenous breed focus but does not embed community knowledge or commons breed stewardship practices
T1_5.note: Gokul Mission's indigenous breed mandate is implemented through government gaushalas rather than community commons institutions.

T1_6.note: No community governance layer. Mission is implemented by state AHD through government gaushalas and breeding centers.

T2_1.note: 60% locality concentration for beneficiaries falls below ≥0.70 pastoral commons threshold. Mission reach is geographically dispersed.

T2_2.note: SC/ST incidence estimated at 20%; no explicit priority targeting mechanism. Pass:false as no mechanism ensures marginal group priority.

T2_3.note: No community governance institution. Mission operates through government gaushalas and AHD staff with no gram-level commons body.

T2_4.flag: No women's leadership provision in Gokul Mission state implementation governance
T2_4.note: Mission implementation lacks mandated women's participation in governance or preferential targeting for women cattle keepers.

T2_5.flag: Indigenous breed conservation implemented through government gaushalas rather than community commons knowledge systems
T2_5.note: Community traditional knowledge of indigenous cattle breeds is not captured or institutionalized in mission design.

T3_1.note: No income mandate. Mission aims at genetic productivity improvement; household income generation is not a stated objective or monitored target.

T3_2.note: Projected indirect income gain from improved bovine productivity estimated at Rs.1,500/HH/year midpoint. Below ≥5,000 projected threshold.

T3_3.note: No documented income pathway. Genetic improvement benefits are indirect and not connected to any income generation mechanism.

T3_4.note: No offtake mechanism. Mission does not link genetic improvement to any cooperative or market procurement channel.

T3_5.flag: No income outcome tracking; Gokul Mission MIS tracks genetic parameters not household income
T3_5.note: Mission monitoring tracks genetic improvement indicators but has no framework for household income outcome measurement.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule. Gokul Mission has no connection to grazing commons management or sustainable pasture extraction.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable to this genetic improvement mission.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund. Government gaushala maintenance depends on recurring annual AHD budget provision.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Mission creates no obligation for shared resource sustainability.

T4_6.flag: No community governance for genetic diversity sustainability or indigenous breed commons stewardship
T4_6.note: Indigenous breed conservation lacks community governance foundation; entirely dependent on government institution management.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Rashtriya Gokul Mission — Operational Guidelines, DAHD Ministry
Rastriya Gokul Mission — Annual Implementation Report 2023-24
Chhattisgarh Gokul Mission State Implementation Plan — AHD

schemes.name: Mukhyamantri Cow Shed Scheme
schemes.objective: Provide financial subsidy for construction of cattle shelters, enabling farmers to establish improved housing for dairy cattle.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (private cattle shelter on individual land); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.05; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0; Delta-Y: negligible (infrastructure, not income); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Transform to Community Cattle Housing Commons: Establish village-level community cattle facilities for 50-100 cattle; gram committee manages facilities
Commons Governance: Individual farmers contribute cattle and management labor; shelter becomes commons infrastructure
Income Integration: Community facility enables cooperative dairy operation; shared milk processing/storage
Sustainability: O&M fund (15% of construction cost); annual facility maintenance audit by gram committee
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Private cattle shelter on individual land is a private capital asset; no commons dimension
STRUCTURAL: T3.1 — Infrastructure subsidy creates no income mandate; cattle shed alone does not generate income
MISSING: T2.2 — No SC/ST priority mechanism; cow shed subsidy typically captured by better-off farmers

T1_1.note: Cattle shelter constructed on individual farmer's private land — a private capital asset with no commons dimension.

T1_2.note: No commons use right. Cattle shed is individual private property with no shared access or community tenure.

T1_3.note: Less than 5% of expenditure relates to any shared resource; over 95% funds private construction on individual landholdings.

T1_4.note: Infrastructure benefit is fully excludable to individual farmer. No non-excludable commons benefit generated.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of traditional community cattle housing commons or shared livestock infrastructure practices
T1_5.note: Scheme focuses on individual private infrastructure without recognizing traditional shared cattle management arrangements.

T1_6.note: No community governance layer. Scheme is direct individual subsidy with no gram-level institutional involvement.

T2_1.note: 65% locality concentration falls below ≥0.70 pastoral commons threshold. Scheme reaches individual farmers across dispersed locations.

T2_2.note: No SC/ST priority mechanism. Construction subsidies typically captured by land-owning farmers; marginal group incidence estimated at 20%. Pass:false.

T2_3.note: No community governance institution. Scheme is entirely individual farmer-to-department delivery.

T2_4.flag: No women's representation or targeting in cow shed subsidy design
T2_4.note: Scheme has no provision for women's leadership or priority access despite women's primary role in cattle care.

T2_5.flag: No community knowledge integration; infrastructure scheme ignores traditional community cattle housing practices
T2_5.note: Scheme design treats cattle housing as individual engineering problem rather than integrating community collective practices.

T3_1.note: No income mandate. Cow shed subsidy is a capital infrastructure transfer; no income generation target is set or monitored.

T3_2.note: Projected indirect income gain from improved cattle shelter negligible (~Rs.500/HH/year); pure infrastructure not income. Well below ≥5,000 threshold. Provisional.

T3_3.note: No income pathway. Cattle shed is infrastructure; income depends entirely on unlinked individual dairy operations.

T3_4.note: No offtake mechanism. Shed construction is not linked to any market, cooperative, or procurement channel.

T3_5.flag: No income tracking; cow shed scheme has no MIS connecting infrastructure to household income outcomes
T3_5.note: Scheme has no mechanism to verify whether cattle shed construction translates to any measurable income improvement.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule. Cow shed subsidy has no connection to any grazing commons management or resource sustainability obligation.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable to this private infrastructure scheme.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund. Shed maintenance is entirely farmer's individual responsibility after subsidy; no collective maintenance provision.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Scheme creates no resource sustainability obligation.

T4_6.flag: No community governance for infrastructure sustainability
T4_6.note: No community governance mechanism for maintenance or sustainability of any scheme component.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Mukhyamantri Gaushan Palan Yojana — Chhattisgarh AHD Scheme Order
Chhattisgarh State Budget 2024-25 — Mukhyamantri Cow Shed Scheme Allocation
Cattle Shelter Infrastructure Schemes — DAHD Best Practice Report 2023

schemes.name: Livestock Health & Disease Control
schemes.objective: Implement disease surveillance, vaccination campaigns, and health monitoring programs across state livestock population, preventing livestock epidemics and productivity loss.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (healthcare to private livestock); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.15; T1.4=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (public health service); Delta-Y: Rs.2,000-3,000/year (loss prevention); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Note: This is a PUBLIC HEALTH SERVICE SCHEME. Falls outside direct SOC scope.
SOC Convergence: Integrate disease surveillance with grazing commons monitoring; condition priority vaccination access on Grazing Committee membership
Community Health Workers: Train community livestock health monitors (one per 200 animals per Grazing Committee)
Commons Data Integration: Animal health data feeds into commons sustainability dashboard
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T1.1 — Disease control treats private livestock; inherently private good public health service
MISSING: T2.2 — No explicit SC/ST priority in vaccination campaign targeting
MISSING: T3.1 — Public health service has no income mandate by design; loss prevention ≠ income generation

T1_1.note: Livestock disease control services protect individually-owned private livestock; a public health service delivering private benefit.

T1_2.note: No commons use right. Vaccination and disease treatment benefits accrue to individual livestock owners; no shared commons access.

T1_3.note: Approximately 15% of expenditure relates to shared surveillance infrastructure and mobile disease monitoring; primary benefit is private animal protection.

T1_4.note: Disease control benefit accrues to individual animal owner despite herd immunity externalities. No direct non-excludable commons benefit.

T1_5.flag: No integration of traditional livestock disease management knowledge or community herbal medicine practices
T1_5.note: Modern disease control protocol excludes traditional community knowledge of livestock disease prevention and management.

T1_6.note: No community governance structure. Disease control is government-administered with no gram-level institution involved.

T2_1.note: 85% coverage at village level; very high geographic reach. T1 failure and T2_2 failure make T2 overall fail.

T2_2.note: SC/ST demographic incidence ~50% but no explicit priority targeting in vaccination campaigns. Pass:false as no mechanism ensures priority access.

T2_3.note: No community governance body for disease control program. Entirely government-staff administered.

T2_4.flag: No women's community health worker program in disease control scheme
T2_4.note: Scheme lacks any provision for women's leadership in community livestock health governance.

T2_5.flag: No integration of traditional disease prevention knowledge in vaccination or extension program design
T2_5.note: Disease control program relies entirely on modern protocols without incorporating traditional community knowledge.

T3_1.note: No income mandate. Disease control is a public health service; loss-prevention savings are indirect and untracked at household level.

T3_2.note: Projected disease-prevention savings midpoint Rs.2,500/HH/year. Falls below ≥5,000 projected income threshold.

T3_3.note: No income pathway. Disease prevention is a risk mitigation input; no income generation mechanism is formalized.

T3_4.note: No offtake mechanism. Disease control does not connect to any market or cooperative procurement channel.

T3_5.flag: No income outcome tracking; disease-prevention benefits not measured as household income equivalents
T3_5.note: Scheme monitoring tracks disease incidence and coverage rates but has no framework for household income outcome measurement.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule. Disease control has no connection to grazing commons management or sustainable resource extraction.

T4_2.note: No natural commons resource managed; rho ratio undefined and not applicable to this public health service scheme.

T4_3.note: No ecological health monitoring of any shared resource. Score 0 below required threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund for disease control infrastructure. Veterinary equipment and cold chain maintenance depends on recurring annual budget.

T4_5.note: No stewardship mechanism. Disease control creates no shared resource sustainability obligation.

T4_6.flag: No community governance for animal health monitoring linked to commons sustainability
T4_6.note: No community governance mechanism linking disease surveillance data to commons health monitoring or sustainability governance.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Livestock Health & Disease Control Programme — DAHD National Guidelines
National Animal Disease Control Programme — Ministry Implementation Report 2023-24
Chhattisgarh AHD — Livestock Disease Surveillance Budget 2024-25

schemes.name: Pastureland Development (Nascent Initiative)
schemes.objective: Develop and improve community grazing lands through pasture seeding, water points, and fodder production, supporting sustainable pastoral livelihoods.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (government/common grazing land); T1.2=1 (community use right); T1.3~0.70 (≥70% on commons pasture restoration); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.80 (pastoral village beneficiaries); T2.2~0.55 (SC/ST dominant); T2.3=1 (Grazing Committee governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no livelihood income mandated — improvement only); Delta-Y: 0 (no income target set); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no mandatory grazing limits); T4.2: undefined; T4.3=0; T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Livelihood Mandate: Every project must project livestock income ≥₹5,000/HH/year; annual income tracking as condition for continued development support
Sustainable Grazing Limits: Set carrying capacity per pasture block; codify E/R ≤1.2; link pasture access to compliance
Monitoring System: Quarterly pasture health assessments; grazing pressure tracking via mobile app — estimated cost ₹6-10 Cr, 12-18 months
O&M Fund: Earmark 10% of capital for 5-year pastureland maintenance; mandatory pasture seeding post-grazing ≥40% annually
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T3.1 — No livelihood income mandate; development focus without income target
STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No mandatory grazing carrying-capacity rule
MISSING: T3.5 — No income outcome tracking

T1_1.note: Scheme targets government/commons grazing land — a natural commons resource. Pastureland development directly invests in shared grazing commons.

T1_2.note: Community use right for developed pastureland is established through Grazing Committee; right is formalized at community level rather than through statutory FRA.

T1_3.note: 70% of scheme expenditure flows to commons pastureland restoration and infrastructure. Passes ≥0.50 threshold comfortably.

T1_4.note: Pastureland benefit is fully non-excludable for all pastoral households in the village. Score 2 reflects strong non-excludability of restored commons.

T1_5.note: Traditional pastoral grazing practices and seasonal grazing routes are recognized in Grazing Committee governance structure of the scheme.

T1_6.note: Grazing Committee is constituted as a mandatory governance institution providing explicit community management layer for pastureland development.

T2_1.note: 80% of beneficiaries are pastoral village households directly dependent on restored grazing commons. Clearly passes ≥0.70 threshold under pastoral definition.

T2_2.note: SC/ST communities are explicitly dominant beneficiaries of pastureland development; explicit pastoral community priority mechanism established. Passes ≥0.30 with targeting.

T2_3.note: Grazing Committee is constituted and functional, providing operational community governance for pastureland management and access.

T2_4.note: Women's participation in Grazing Committee is encouraged; some districts mandate women members in committee leadership for pastureland governance.

T2_5.flag: No systematic intergenerational knowledge transfer for traditional grazing management practices despite pastoral community focus
T2_5.note: Scheme does not embed mechanisms to document or transmit traditional seasonal grazing knowledge across generations of pastoral communities.

T3_1.note: No livelihood income mandate. Scheme objective is pastureland improvement; no household income generation target is set or monitored.

T3_2.note: No income target set; Delta-Y is effectively zero as scheme does not mandate or track income generation from improved pasturelands.

T3_3.note: No documented income pathway. Scheme develops pasture but creates no mechanism to convert improved productivity to household income.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake mechanism. Pastureland improvement is not linked to any market or cooperative procurement channel.

T3_5.flag: No income outcome tracking; no MIS linking pastureland improvement to household income measurement
T3_5.note: Critical gap: scheme has no framework to measure whether improved pasturelands translate to actual household income improvement.

T4_1.note: No binding grazing carrying-capacity rule established despite being a pastureland scheme. Development investment is not protected by mandatory sustainable grazing limits.

T4_2.note: Pasture extraction-to-replenishment ratio is undefined and unmonitored. No rho measurement framework exists despite natural grazing commons being the scheme resource.

T4_3.note: No pasture health monitoring framework established. Scheme develops pasture but does not institutionalize ongoing ecological health tracking.

T4_4.note: No O&M fund earmarked for pastureland maintenance. Pasture seeding and water point maintenance depend on future budget allocations without dedicated provision.

T4_5.note: No formal stewardship mechanism beyond Grazing Committee formation. No obligation for ongoing maintenance or replenishment after development phase.

T4_6.flag: Grazing Committee lacks binding enforcement powers for sustainable grazing limits; community governance present but not empowered
T4_6.note: Grazing Committee exists for management but has no binding authority to enforce carrying-capacity rules or penalize overgrazing.

T4_7.note: Animal Husbandry Department not in climate-stressed zone; T4.7 not triggered.

references.title: Chhattisgarh Pastureland Development Initiative — State AHD Guidelines
National Livestock Mission — Pasture and Fodder Development Component, DAHD
Degraded Pastureland Restoration in Chhattisgarh — FES Field Assessment Report 2023
